learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a well-founded knowledge of the key ideas and issues in the field of research methods.
Demonstrate acquired conceptual and analytical capacities, along with an ability to apply these skills as appropriate.
Develop intellectual tools and skills that will make you more competitive in your future professional activities in a variety of fields and positions.

assessments.weighting: <dd>30% Best two of three quiz marks </dd>
assessments.task_description:
There will be three in-class quizzes. The best two are counted towards your final grade and worth 15% each.

Quizzes will be 50 minutes long, hand written, and consist of multiple choice and short answer questions.

Quiz 1: Week 4

Quiz 2: Week 9

Quiz 3: Week 12

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (Al) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of Al or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
The Research Design Paper (2500 words, not including references or appendices) requires students to prepare a concise research proposal for a Masters-level project. The paper should outline the research question, relevant literature, proposed methods, and key ethical and practical considerations shaping the design of the project.

Artificial Intelligence (Al) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use Al and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of Al or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative Al or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You will submit your paper electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Conducted during exam period.

•Invigilated in person.

•Closed Book examination - specified written materials permitted

•A 120 minute examination containing short answer questions and an extended answer essay question covering all the course learning material delivered for a possible 40 marks.

You will have a choice of a list of short answer questions and will choose two (2) to answer (approximately 250 words each)

You will have a choice of a list of essay questions and will choose one (1) one to answer (approximately 1000 words)

This assessment task is to be conducted in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1 – What is research design for? Questions, claims, and contribution

This week introduces the course and considers what research design is for in graduate study. Students will examine how research questions, claims, and contributions are formed, and why they matter for building a viable project.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 1 – What is research design for? Questions, claims, and contribution
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1 – What is research design for? Questions, claims, and contribution This week introduces the course and considers what research design is for in graduate study. Students will examine how research questions, claims, and contributions are formed, and why they matter for building a viable project.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 – Research problems, puzzles, and the shaping of a question

This week focuses on how broad interests are turned into researchable problems and puzzles. Students will work on refining research questions so they are clear, focused, and feasible.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 2 – Research problems, puzzles, and the shaping of a question
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2 – Research problems, puzzles, and the shaping of a question This week focuses on how broad interests are turned into researchable problems and puzzles. Students will work on refining research questions so they are clear, focused, and feasible.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3 – Literature reviews, concepts, and positioning a project

This week examines how literature reviews help locate a project within existing scholarship. Students will consider how concepts are defined and how a project can be positioned in relation to relevant debates

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 3 – Literature reviews, concepts, and positioning a project
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3 – Literature reviews, concepts, and positioning a project This week examines how literature reviews help locate a project within existing scholarship. Students will consider how concepts are defined and how a project can be positioned in relation to relevant debates

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4 – Document analysis, corpus selection and coding logic

This week introduces documents as a source of evidence in social research. Students will consider how to select a document corpus and how qualitative coding can be used to organise and interpret documentary material.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 4 – Document analysis, corpus selection and coding logic
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4 – Document analysis, corpus selection and coding logic This week introduces documents as a source of evidence in social research. Students will consider how to select a document corpus and how qualitative coding can be used to organise and interpret documentary material.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5 – Introduction to qualitative coding

This week provides an introduction to the logic and practice of qualitative coding. Students will work with sample materials to identify themes, categories, and patterns in a systematic way.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 5 – Introduction to qualitative coding This week provides an introduction to the logic and practice of qualitative coding. Students will work with sample materials to identify themes, categories, and patterns in a systematic way.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6 – Course Reading Week

No seminar this week. Students are expected to use this time to consolidate course readings and prepare for the second half of semester.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6 – Course Reading Week No seminar this week. Students are expected to use this time to consolidate course readings and prepare for the second half of semester.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7 – Online lecture: Qualitative research design: interviews, observation, documents, fieldwork

This pre-recorded online lecture introduces key approaches to qualitative research design. It considers how different forms of qualitative evidence are generated and the kinds of claims they can support. There will be no in person class this week.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 7 – Online lecture: Qualitative research design: interviews, observation, documents, fieldwork
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7 – Online lecture: Qualitative research design: interviews, observation, documents, fieldwork This pre-recorded online lecture introduces key approaches to qualitative research design. It considers how different forms of qualitative evidence are generated and the kinds of claims they can support. There will be no in person class this week.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8 – Interviews and field notes

This week focuses on interviews as a method of data collection and on the role of field notes in qualitative research. Students will consider how interview design, note-taking, and reflection shape the quality of research evidence.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 8 – Interviews and field notes This week focuses on interviews as a method of data collection and on the role of field notes in qualitative research. Students will consider how interview design, note-taking, and reflection shape the quality of research evidence.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9 – Ethics, power, positionality, and risk

This week examines ethical issues in research design, including informed consent, confidentiality, harm, and researcher responsibility. It also considers how power, positionality, and risk shape the conduct of research.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 9 – Ethics, power, positionality, and risk This week examines ethical issues in research design, including informed consent, confidentiality, harm, and researcher responsibility. It also considers how power, positionality, and risk shape the conduct of research.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10 – Quantitative reasoning for non-specialists

This week introduces quantitative reasoning for students who are not specialising in quantitative methods. It focuses on how to read, interpret, and critically assess quantitative evidence in social research. This week will be a make-up class sometime during the week because of the public holiday.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 10 – Quantitative reasoning for non-specialists This week introduces quantitative reasoning for students who are not specialising in quantitative methods. It focuses on how to read, interpret, and critically assess quantitative evidence in social research. This week will be a make-up class sometime during the week because of the public holiday.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11 – Case studies, historical analysis and process tracing

This week explores case study research and within-case analysis. Students will be introduced to historical analysis and process tracing as ways of explaining political and social outcomes.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 11 – Case studies, historical analysis and process tracing
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 11 – Case studies, historical analysis and process tracing This week explores case study research and within-case analysis. Students will be introduced to historical analysis and process tracing as ways of explaining political and social outcomes.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 – Mixed methods, triangulation, and combining forms of evidence

This week considers how qualitative and quantitative approaches can be combined in a single research design. Students will examine triangulation and the strengths and limitations of mixed methods research.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 12 – Mixed methods, triangulation, and combining forms of evidence
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 12 – Mixed methods, triangulation, and combining forms of evidence This week considers how qualitative and quantitative approaches can be combined in a single research design. Students will examine triangulation and the strengths and limitations of mixed methods research.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13 – Conclusion: AI and research practice

This final week reflects on the role of AI and digital tools in contemporary research practice. It also reviews the course as a whole and considers how students can apply its lessons to their own research projects.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 13 – Conclusion: AI and research practice This final week reflects on the role of AI and digital tools in contemporary research practice. It also reviews the course as a whole and considers how students can apply its lessons to their own research projects.
